text:
MRI NECK WITH AND WITHOUT CONTRAST
ANNUAL SURVEILLANCE IMAGING — 2-YEAR POST-TREATMENT

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Margaret Chen, 54-year-old female with history of HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, T2N1M0, treated with definitive concurrent cisplatin-IMRT completing approximately 24 months prior. Surveillance MRI for locoregional monitoring. Prior PET-CT and MRI at 12 months showed no evidence of recurrent disease. Patient currently asymptomatic.

TECHNIQUE:
Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the neck performed on a 3.0 Tesla system utilizing a dedicated head and neck coil. Sequences obtained include axial and coronal T1-weighted, axial T2-weighted with fat saturation, axial and coronal short tau inversion recovery (STIR), and axial and coronal T1-weighted fat-saturated sequences with gadolinium contrast (gadopentetate dimeglumine, 15 mL IV). Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) performed with ADC mapping. Comparison made to prior MRI neck obtained at approximately 12 months post-treatment and 18-month clinical visit records.

FINDINGS:

Base of Tongue / Oropharynx (Primary Site):
The left base of tongue demonstrates stable, chronic post-radiation mucosal and submucosal changes. There is mild, diffuse T2 signal heterogeneity and subtle mucosal thickening along the left glossotonsillar sulcus and inferior left base of tongue, which is unchanged from the prior 12-month MRI. No discrete soft tissue mass, no focal area of restricted diffusion suspicious for viable tumor, and no abnormal nodular or mass-like enhancement is identified. The region of prior radiation-related mucosal thickening has, if anything, marginally diminished in extent compared to the 12-month study. The right base of tongue, vallecula, and lingual tonsil tissue appear within normal limits for a patient of this age with this treatment history. No involvement of the epiglottis or pre-epiglottic space. The intrinsic tongue musculature is intact bilaterally with no infiltrative signal abnormality.

Cervical Lymph Nodes:
Previously described residual soft tissue in the left level IIA station, measuring 8 mm at the 12-month study, is no longer identifiable as a discrete nodal structure on the current examination. The left level IIA region shows only streaky fibrofatty changes consistent with post-treatment involution and scarring. No pathologically enlarged or abnormally enhancing lymph nodes are identified in any cervical nodal station bilaterally — including levels I through V and the retropharyngeal and retrostyloid spaces. No new lymphadenopathy on either side.

Soft Tissues of the Neck:
The post-radiation soft tissue changes in the bilateral neck are stable compared to prior examination. There is mild thickening and T2 hyperintensity of the subcutaneous and superficial muscular fascia in the bilateral neck at the radiation field margins, reflecting chronic radiation-induced fibrotic change. No subcutaneous edema or skin thickening to suggest active radiation toxicity. No new soft tissue masses or collections. The floor of mouth and submandibular spaces are unremarkable. The parapharyngeal spaces are patent and symmetric.

Mandible and Skull Base:
The mandibular cortex is intact without cortical disruption, signal abnormality, or periosteal reaction that would suggest radiation-induced osteonecrosis. Bone marrow signal of the mandible is within normal limits without focal replacement or abnormal T1 hypointensity. The skull base is intact. No erosive or infiltrative bony changes identified.

Salivary Glands:
The parotid glands are mildly diminished in volume bilaterally, more pronounced on the left, consistent with expected radiation-related atrophic changes in this clinical context. No discrete masses or ductal dilatation. The submandibular glands are mildly reduced in volume bilaterally. The sublingual glands appear normal.

Vascular Structures:
The bilateral carotid arteries are patent with flow-voids preserved on all sequences. There is mild wall thickening and focal low T1/T2 signal changes along the medial walls of the bilateral common and internal carotid arteries in the mid-to-upper neck, consistent with mild atherosclerotic plaque and radiation-associated intimal changes. These findings are stable without significant interval progression compared to the prior study. No carotid stenosis is identified. The jugular veins are patent bilaterally.

Thyroid Gland:
The thyroid gland is mildly decreased in volume compared to pre-treatment documentation, compatible with radiation-related atrophy. No discrete nodules or focal signal abnormality. No thyroid masses.

Larynx and Hypopharynx:
The laryngeal framework is intact. The true and false vocal cords appear symmetric with normal signal. The arytenoids are normal. The pyriform sinuses are open and symmetric. No masses or abnormal enhancement in the larynx or hypopharynx.

Additional Observations:
DWI/ADC mapping: No foci of restricted diffusion at the primary site or in any cervical nodal station to suggest residual or recurrent viable tumor. Signal characteristics throughout the examined region are consistent with expected late post-treatment changes.

IMPRESSION:
1. No evidence of locoregional recurrence at the left base of tongue primary site or in the bilateral cervical nodal chains. The treatment bed at the left base of tongue shows stable post-radiation mucosal and soft tissue changes, without any new mass, abnormal enhancement, or restricted diffusion to suggest residual or recurrent malignancy.

2. The previously noted small residual soft tissue in the left level IIA nodal station (8 mm on 12-month study) has completely resolved, with only fibrofatty scar tissue now present in this region.

3. Stable radiation-related soft tissue and salivary gland changes in the bilateral neck, without progression or new findings of concern.

4. Mild stable atherosclerotic and radiation-associated intimal changes of the bilateral carotid arteries, unchanged from prior study.

5. No evidence of radiation-induced osteonecrosis of the mandible or adjacent structures.

Overall, these findings are reassuring at the 2-year post-treatment interval and are consistent with durable complete locoregional response to prior chemoradiotherapy.
